questions.question: The doctrine of frustration discharges a contract where:

options.A: One party finds performance more difficult or expensive than anticipated
options.B: A supervening event, after contract formation, makes performance impossible, illegal, or fundamentally different from what was undertaken, through no fault of either party
options.C: The subject matter of the contract is damaged after formation but before the risk passes to the buyer
options.D: A party cannot perform because they have allocated their resources to a more profitable contract

questions.explanation: Frustration discharges both parties automatically from future performance obligations where a supervening (post-formation) event: (1) makes performance impossible, illegal, or fundamentally different from what was undertaken; (2) was not caused by either party; and (3) was not provided for in the contract. The doctrine was developed to address the harsh consequences of the rule that parties are absolutely bound by their contractual undertakings, modified from the absolute liability principle of Paradine v Jane [1647] following Taylor v Caldwell [1863].

questions.question: The foundational case establishing the doctrine of frustration is:

options.A: Paradine v Jane [1647]
options.B: Taylor v Caldwell [1863] (QB)
options.C: Davis Contractors Ltd v Fareham UDC [1956] (HL)
options.D: Krell v Henry [1903] (CA)

questions.explanation: Taylor v Caldwell [1863] is the foundational authority for the modern doctrine of frustration. Blackburn J held that where a music hall was destroyed by fire before the contracted performances took place, the contract was discharged — the implied condition was that the thing should continue to exist. The case departed from the prior absolute liability rule in Paradine v Jane [1647] (which held parties absolutely bound by their promises even if performance became impossible) and introduced the concept of an implied condition as the theoretical basis for frustration. Later cases moved away from implied conditions to objective construction.

questions.question: In Davis Contractors Ltd v Fareham UDC [1956] (HL), the House of Lords reformulated the test for frustration. Lord Radcliffe's test holds that frustration occurs when:

options.A: The court implies a term that excuses the non-performing party from their obligations
options.B: Performance has become significantly more onerous and expensive than anticipated at the time of contracting
options.C: The situation has, without fault of either party, become radically different from what was undertaken at the time of contract — such that it would be unjust to hold either party bound
options.D: A supervening event has made literal performance of the contract legally prohibited

questions.explanation: In Davis Contractors [1956], Lord Radcliffe rejected the implied term theory (finding it fictitious) and formulated the test as: 'frustration occurs wherever the law recognises that without default of either party a contractual obligation has become incapable of being performed because the circumstances in which performance is called for would render it a thing radically different from that which was undertaken by the contract.' The emphasis is on whether the obligation itself has become radically different — not merely harder or more expensive. The law's role is to identify this objective change in circumstances.

questions.question: The Coronation Cases — including Krell v Henry [1903] and Herne Bay Steamboat Co v Hutton [1903] — established which distinction?

options.A: Frustration applies only where both parties anticipated the specific event that occurred
options.B: Frustration of purpose applies where the common basis of the contract has been destroyed, even if literal performance is still possible — but it does not apply where only one party's purpose fails while the other retains benefit
options.C: Coronation events can never found a frustration claim because they are foreseeable royal events
options.D: Frustration applies equally to all contracts in a series of linked transactions

questions.explanation: The Coronation Cases established the doctrine of frustration of purpose. In Krell v Henry [1903], a room hired to watch the coronation procession (which was cancelled due to the King's illness) was held frustrated — the common purpose of the contract had been destroyed even though literal performance (use of the room) was possible. In Herne Bay [1903], a boat hired partly to view the naval review but also for a general excursion was not frustrated — the cancellation of the review did not destroy the whole purpose because the excursion purpose survived. The distinction is whether the whole common foundation of the contract was destroyed.

questions.question: Frustration will NOT operate to discharge a contract where:

options.A: Performance has become significantly more expensive but not impossible
options.B: The frustrating event was caused by the fault or election of one of the parties (self-induced frustration)
options.C: The frustrating event occurred shortly after the contract was made
options.D: Only A and B are correct

questions.explanation: Two important limitations on frustration are: (1) Increased expense or difficulty — mere increased cost or hardship does not frustrate a contract: Davis Contractors [1956] (building contract took much longer and cost more due to labour shortage — not frustrated); and (2) Self-induced frustration — a party cannot rely on frustration if the frustrating event was caused by their own conduct, choice, or negligence: Maritime National Fish Ltd v Ocean Trawlers [1935] (PC). Both A and B are therefore correct limitations on the doctrine.

questions.question: In Maritime National Fish Ltd v Ocean Trawlers [1935] (PC), the principle of self-induced frustration was applied. The case held that:

options.A: A fishing company could rely on frustration where the government refused to grant it enough fishing licences to operate its fleet
options.B: A fishing company could not rely on frustration where, having been granted fewer licences than it had vessels, it chose to allocate those licences to other vessels and claimed the contract with the owner of the unregistered vessel was frustrated
options.C: A government refusal to grant licences is always a frustrating event for commercial fishing contracts
options.D: Frustration applies only where the government's action was unlawful

questions.explanation: In Maritime National Fish [1935], the company had five trawlers but was granted only three licences. It chose to allocate the licences to three trawlers other than the Ocean Trawlers vessel. The Privy Council held that the frustration was self-induced: the company's choice determined which vessels would be licensed. Having freely chosen not to use the licence for Ocean Trawlers, the company could not claim the contract with the vessel's owner was frustrated by lack of a licence — the situation resulted from their own election, not from an external supervening event.

questions.question: Which of the following events is LEAST likely to constitute frustration?

options.A: The key performance venue is destroyed by fire before the agreed concerts
options.B: A war breaks out making performance of a trading contract with a business in an enemy country illegal
options.C: A building contract takes twice as long to complete as anticipated due to bad weather
options.D: The sole performer contracted to give a recital becomes permanently incapacitated due to illness

questions.explanation: Mere delay or increased difficulty does not generally frustrate a contract: Davis Contractors [1956] — a building contract that took 22 months instead of the expected 8 months due to labour shortages was held not frustrated. The contract remained capable of performance, even though it was more onerous than anticipated. Options A (physical destruction — Taylor v Caldwell [1863]), B (supervening illegality — Fibrosa [1943]), and D (personal incapacity in a personal services contract — Robinson v Davison [1871]) are all established categories of frustrating events.

questions.question: Supervening illegality as a ground of frustration arises where:

options.A: A party discovers their counterparty was not legally authorised to enter the contract at the time of formation
options.B: A change in the law after formation of the contract makes performance of the contractual obligation illegal
options.C: One party refuses to perform on the grounds that performance would be commercially inappropriate
options.D: A new regulatory requirement increases the cost of performance significantly

questions.explanation: Supervening illegality occurs where a change in the law after the contract is formed makes performance of the contractual obligation illegal. In Denny Mott & Dickson Ltd v James B Fraser & Co Ltd [1944] (HL), wartime government regulations that made trading in certain timber illegal frustrated a timber supply agreement. The performance was no longer lawful — it was impossible to perform without committing an offence. This is distinct from initial illegality (where the contract was illegal at formation), which renders it void ab initio rather than frustrated.

questions.question: At common law, what was the consequence of frustration in relation to money paid before the frustrating event?

options.A: All money paid before the frustrating event could be recovered
options.B: Under the rule in Chandler v Webster [1904], money paid before the frustrating event could not be recovered — the loss lay where it fell
options.C: All money paid could be recovered under the quantum meruit principle
options.D: Money paid could only be recovered if the frustrating event occurred within 30 days of payment

questions.explanation: At common law (Chandler v Webster [1904] (CA)), money paid before the frustrating event could not be recovered and the obligation to pay money already due but not paid was not discharged. The loss simply lay where it fell at the moment of frustration. This produced harsh results — illustrated in the Coronation Cases where hirers had paid in advance for rooms to view cancelled coronation processions and lost their money. This injustice was remedied by the Law Reform (Frustrated Contracts) Act 1943 in England (and the equivalent Civil Law (Miscellaneous Provisions) Act 2008 in Ireland).

questions.question: In Fibrosa Spolka Akcyjna v Fairbairn Lawson Combe Barbour Ltd [1943] (HL), the House of Lords overruled Chandler v Webster and held that advance payments could be recovered where:

options.A: The frustrating event was caused by a third party
options.B: There had been a total failure of consideration — the payer had received absolutely nothing in return for the money paid
options.C: The frustrated contract was for the sale of goods
options.D: Both parties were aware of the risk of the frustrating event when the contract was made

questions.explanation: In Fibrosa [1943], the House of Lords held that advance payments could be recovered where there had been a total failure of consideration — the payer had received absolutely nothing in return for the advance payment (no part of the performance had been rendered). The recovery was in unjust enrichment (quasi-contract), not in contract. The decision was limited to total failure of consideration — where partial performance had occurred, recovery was not available. This gap (no recovery for partial failure) was addressed by the Law Reform (Frustrated Contracts) Act 1943 in England.

questions.question: Under the Civil Law (Miscellaneous Provisions) Act 2008 (Ireland), which enacts provisions equivalent to the Law Reform (Frustrated Contracts) Act 1943 (England), the effect on prepayments on frustration is:

options.A: All prepayments are automatically repaid on frustration, regardless of expenses incurred by the recipient
options.B: Prepayments are recoverable and sums due but unpaid cease to be payable, but the court may allow the payee to retain or recover expenses incurred before frustration, not exceeding the prepayment
options.C: No prepayments are recoverable — the legislation confirms the common law position in Chandler v Webster
options.D: Prepayments are only recoverable where the frustrating event was unforeseeable

questions.explanation: The Civil Law (Miscellaneous Provisions) Act 2008 (Part 3) is the Irish equivalent of the Law Reform (Frustrated Contracts) Act 1943. It provides that: (1) money paid before frustration is recoverable; (2) money due but unpaid ceases to be payable; but (3) a payee who has incurred expenses before frustration in performing the contract may retain (or recover) those expenses out of the prepayment, up to the amount of the prepayment. The court has a discretion as to what is just in the circumstances. This addresses the injustice of both Chandler v Webster (payer loses all) and the counter-injustice of requiring a recipient who has done work to return all payments.

questions.question: Under the Civil Law (Miscellaneous Provisions) Act 2008, where a party has conferred a valuable benefit on the other party before the frustrating event, they may claim:

options.A: The full contract price for the benefit conferred
options.B: A just sum, not exceeding the value of the benefit conferred, at the court's discretion
options.C: The reasonable market value of the benefit, assessed as of the date of the contract
options.D: Only the actual cost to the provider of conferring the benefit

questions.explanation: Under the 2008 Act (following the 1943 Act), where a party has conferred a valuable benefit on the other before frustration (other than the payment of money), they may apply to the court for a 'just sum' in respect of that benefit. The court may award a sum not exceeding the value of the benefit to the receiving party. The court exercises a discretion in determining what is just in all the circumstances — it is not automatically the market value, the cost of provision, or the contract price. The effect of the frustrating event on the value of the benefit may be relevant (BP Exploration v Hunt [1982] (CA England)).

questions.question: A force majeure clause in a commercial contract differs from the common law doctrine of frustration in that:

options.A: Force majeure applies only to acts of God; frustration applies to any external event
options.B: A force majeure clause is a contractual provision expressly allocating the risk of specified events; the common law doctrine of frustration applies in the absence of such a provision
options.C: Force majeure automatically terminates the contract; frustration requires a court order
options.D: Force majeure excuses one party only; frustration discharges both parties

questions.explanation: A force majeure clause is a term of the contract that expressly allocates the risk of specified events (typically listed: war, natural disaster, pandemic, strike, government action, etc.) by excusing performance or suspending obligations in those circumstances. It is a contractual mechanism that displaces the common law doctrine of frustration in its sphere of operation. The common law doctrine of frustration applies as a default rule in the absence of such contractual provision, or where the triggering event is not covered by the force majeure clause. A party seeking to rely on a force majeure clause must satisfy its specific terms.

questions.question: In the Irish context, the COVID-19 pandemic was raised as a potential frustrating event in commercial contracts. Which of the following most accurately describes the approach courts have taken?

options.A: Courts universally accepted COVID-19 as a frustrating event for all commercial contracts
options.B: Courts have been cautious — a contract is not frustrated merely because performance has become more difficult or expensive due to pandemic restrictions; it requires radical difference in the nature of the obligation
options.C: COVID-19 was declared a national emergency making all commercial contracts automatically frustrated
options.D: Courts refused to consider COVID-19 as a frustrating event because it was foreseeable from global pandemic risk

questions.explanation: Courts (in Ireland and England) have approached COVID-19 frustration claims cautiously, consistent with the Davis Contractors [1956] test. The pandemic itself is not automatically a frustrating event for any particular contract — it depends on the specific contract and how pandemic restrictions affected the specific obligations undertaken. Where a contract's performance became temporarily delayed but was still possible, courts generally declined to find frustration. Where performance was made permanently impossible (e.g., a venue licence permanently suspended), frustration might be established. Foreseeability and force majeure clauses have also been relevant factors.

questions.question: The effect of frustration on a contract is:

options.A: The contract is void ab initio — as if it had never been made
options.B: The contract is discharged automatically at the point of the frustrating event — future obligations are extinguished, past obligations that have already accrued are not affected (subject to the 2008 Act)
options.C: The contract is suspended until the frustrating event resolves
options.D: The contract is discharged only from the date a court makes a declaration of frustration

questions.explanation: Frustration operates automatically — once the frustrating event occurs, the contract is discharged without any election or court order. The discharge takes effect from the moment of the frustrating event. The consequences for past obligations depend on the Civil Law (Miscellaneous Provisions) Act 2008 in Ireland (which modifies the common law position that losses lie where they fall). Crucially, frustration does not operate ab initio — it does not void the contract from formation. Obligations that have already accrued and been performed before the frustrating event remain binding (subject to the statutory adjustment of prepayments).

questions.question: In National Carriers Ltd v Panalpina (Northern) Ltd [1981] (HL), the House of Lords confirmed that:

options.A: The doctrine of frustration never applies to leases of land
options.B: The doctrine of frustration can in principle apply to leases, though in practice it will rarely do so given the proprietary nature of a lease
options.C: Frustration automatically applies to all property leases where the premises become unusable
options.D: Frustration of a lease entitles the tenant to recover all rent paid since the frustrating event

questions.explanation: In National Carriers [1981], the House of Lords overruled the previous position (that frustration could not apply to leases) and held that in principle the doctrine can apply to leases of land. However, the Lords acknowledged that in practice frustration of a lease will be very rare — the proprietary element of a lease (the estate in land) makes it different from purely personal obligations. The lease in that case (where a street was closed for some years making access difficult) was not frustrated — the lease would run for most of its remaining term without the restriction. The case is authority for the narrow principle that frustration can apply to leases.

questions.question: Frustration and breach of contract differ in that:

options.A: Frustration entitles the innocent party to damages; breach does not
options.B: Frustration is an automatic discharge by external event without fault; breach involves a voluntary failure to perform an obligation and gives the innocent party rights to claim damages
options.C: Frustration can be claimed by either party; breach can only be claimed by the innocent party
options.D: Frustration requires a court order; breach operates automatically

questions.explanation: The fundamental distinction is fault and its legal consequences. Frustration is an external supervening event — without fault of either party — that automatically discharges both parties from their future obligations. No damages are available in frustration because there is no wrongdoer. Breach, by contrast, involves a voluntary failure by one party to perform an obligation for which they are responsible — the innocent party acquires rights to damages and, where the breach is sufficiently serious, the right to terminate future obligations. Fault is the dividing line: where a party's own conduct causes the supervening event, the doctrine of self-induced frustration may apply instead of discharging them.

questions.question: A long-term supply contract becomes significantly more expensive to perform due to a dramatic rise in raw material costs. The contract contains no force majeure clause. The supplier claims the contract is frustrated. Is it?

options.A: Yes — a significant increase in cost makes performance fundamentally different from what was agreed
options.B: No — mere increased expense does not frustrate a contract; the obligation remains the same, even if it is now commercially disadvantageous
options.C: It depends on whether the cost increase was foreseeable at the time of contracting
options.D: Yes — the doctrine of frustration protects parties from commercially ruinous performance

questions.explanation: Increased expense, even severe financial hardship, does not frustrate a contract. In Davis Contractors [1956], increased cost and delay were held not to frustrate a building contract. In Tsakiroglou v Noblee Thorl GmbH [1962] (HL), closure of the Suez Canal doubling shipping costs did not frustrate a contract for the sale of groundnuts. The test is whether the obligation itself has become radically different — not whether performance has become harder or more expensive. The parties are expected to assume the risk of price fluctuations in long-term contracts; if they wished to protect against this, they should have included a price adjustment or force majeure clause.

questions.question: The principle that a frustrating event must not have been foreseen (or foreseeable) by the parties at the time of contracting is qualified in that:

options.A: If any risk of the event was foreseeable, the doctrine of frustration is automatically excluded
options.B: Foreseeability is a relevant factor but is not absolutely determinative — a foreseeable event may still frustrate if the parties did not provide for it and its occurrence rendered performance radically different
options.C: The frustrating event must have been completely unforeseeable to establish frustration
options.D: Foreseeability is irrelevant to frustration — only the objective impact of the event matters

questions.explanation: Foreseeability is a relevant consideration in frustration — if an event was foreseeable and the parties did not provide for it, this may suggest they assumed the risk of its occurrence. However, foreseeability is not absolutely determinative: a foreseeable risk that the parties did not explicitly address may still give rise to frustration where the event actually occurs and radically alters the nature of the obligation. In The Eugenia [1964] (CA), Lord Denning acknowledged that the Suez Crisis was foreseeable to some extent but held that the contract should be construed to determine if it allocated the risk. The absence of a force majeure clause does not automatically mean the risk was allocated to the performing party.

questions.question: In Amalgamated Investment and Property Co Ltd v John Walker & Sons Ltd [1977] (CA England), a contract for the sale of a warehouse was affected by the building being listed as a protected structure after exchange of contracts. The Court held:

options.A: The contract was frustrated because listing made redevelopment impossible
options.B: The contract was not frustrated — the building still existed and could still be used; the loss in development value was a risk the buyer had assumed by entering a commercial property transaction
options.C: The contract was frustrated because neither party had contemplated the possibility of listing
options.D: The contract was frustrated because government action rendered commercial use of the building illegal

questions.explanation: In Amalgamated Investment [1977], the Court of Appeal held that the listing of the warehouse (which significantly reduced its development value) did not frustrate the contract. The listing did not make the building's use impossible or even illegal for existing purposes — it simply restricted future development. The buyer had assumed the commercial risk of changes in planning status. This case illustrates that the loss of an expected commercial benefit, even a significant one, does not constitute frustration where the core obligation (transfer of the property) remained capable of performance in its essential character.

questions.question: In Ireland, which legislation governs the consequences of frustrated contracts, replacing the harsh common law position?

options.A: The Frustrated Contracts Act 1988 (Ireland)
options.B: Part 3 of the Civil Law (Miscellaneous Provisions) Act 2008
options.C: The Law Reform (Frustrated Contracts) Act 1943, as applied in Ireland
options.D: The Consumer Rights Act 2022, Part 5

questions.explanation: Part 3 of the Civil Law (Miscellaneous Provisions) Act 2008 is the Irish statutory provision governing the consequences of frustrated contracts, equivalent to the Law Reform (Frustrated Contracts) Act 1943 in England. The 2008 Act provides that: (1) money paid before frustration is recoverable; (2) sums due but unpaid cease to be payable; (3) a payee who has incurred expenses may retain them (up to the prepayment); and (4) a party who has conferred a valuable benefit other than money may recover a 'just sum.' The Act addresses the unfairness of the common law position established in Chandler v Webster [1904] and confirmed by Fibrosa [1943].

questions.question: Which of the following most accurately summarises what CANNOT be recovered under the doctrine of frustration?

options.A: Money paid before the frustrating event
options.B: Expectation losses — the profits either party would have made if the contract had been fully performed
options.C: Expenses incurred in reliance on the contract before the frustrating event
options.D: The value of benefits conferred before the frustrating event

questions.explanation: Frustration does not give rise to a claim for expectation losses (lost profits, loss of bargain). Because frustration operates without fault — neither party is a wrongdoer — there is no damages claim by either party against the other for the profits they would have made had the contract proceeded. This is the fundamental contrast with breach of contract, which does entitle the innocent party to expectation damages. Under the Civil Law (Miscellaneous Provisions) Act 2008, limited recovery of reliance losses (expenses) and money paid is available, but these are restitutionary — not expectation-based.
